summary: Reis contrasts 'givers' who build trust by contributing freely to communities with 'takers' who constantly pitch and monetize every interaction. He argues that giving away most of your work for free and building relationships over time is paradoxically the fastest path to success, while aggressive cold-pitching is a losing strategy in a noisy market.
key_points: Give away 90%+ of what you create for free; the 1:many approach beats 1:1 solicitation
The long game of building audience and trust first is often the shortest path to success
Cold pitches fail because they never answer 'What's in it for me?'
Relationships and trust are prerequisites for getting help from busy people
Community contribution and clear, simple pitches earn attention; interruptions and vague asks get ignored
money_quotes: Ironically, the long game is sometimes the shortest path to success.
If I tried to sell you something every five minutes, you'd tune out. And honestly, I'd feel gross about myself too.
In the end, the people I say yes to are givers. They add more value than they take. And over time, that's what wins.
It feels weird having to remind grown adults about simple things like courtesy, but here we are.
key_insight: Building trust through generous contribution to your community creates more opportunities than aggressive self-promotion ever will.
